treatments.name: Salvage Re-excision + Adjuvant Nivolumab + Ipilimumab

evidence_level.rationale: Surgery remains most effective local control for sinonasal mucosal melanoma per multiple retrospective series. Adjuvant nivo+ipi based on CheckMate-067 mucosal melanoma pooled analysis showing ORR 37-43% and 5-year OS 36%. No dedicated phase 3 for adjuvant ICI in mucosal melanoma, but strong extrapolation from cutaneous melanoma adjuvant data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Salvage surgery achieves locoregional control in 60-70% when combined with adjuvant RT. Adding nivo+ipi addresses the 50-80% recurrence and distant failure rate. 5-year OS 36% with nivo+ipi in advanced mucosal melanoma (vs 17% nivo alone, 7% ipi alone). PD-L1 40% predicts above-average immunotherapy response.

accessibility.rationale: Salvage endoscopic resection available at experienced sinonasal surgery centers. Nivolumab and ipilimumab both FDA approved for melanoma. Combination regimen widely available. Off-label for adjuvant mucosal melanoma setting.

safety_profile.rationale: Nivo+ipi combination has grade 3-4 AEs in 40% of mucosal melanoma patients. Immune-related colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies. Salvage surgery adds operative morbidity (septal perforation, nasal obstruction). Combined toxicity is significant.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 40% is above average for mucosal melanoma (only 44% express PD-L1). Predicts enhanced immunotherapy response. NRAS Q61R does not preclude immunotherapy benefit. Combination nivo+ipi may overcome lower tumor mutational burden typical of mucosal melanoma.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Salvage surgical re-excision provides immediate local disease control, followed by adjuvant nivolumab (anti-PD-1) + ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) dual checkpoint blockade. Nivolumab blocks PD-1/PD-L1 interaction while ipilimumab releases CTLA-4-mediated T-cell suppression. Dual blockade produces complementary immune activation — ipilimumab primes T-cell responses while nivolumab sustains anti-tumor immunity. Critical for mucosal melanoma which has lower TMB and fewer neoantigens than cutaneous melanoma.
biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 expression (positive predicts better response but not required)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — nivolumab + ipilimumab for advanced melanoma (off-label adjuvant for mucosal)

treatments.name: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab (Neoadjuvant/Definitive — Without Surgery)

evidence_level.rationale: CheckMate-067 pooled analysis in mucosal melanoma: ORR 37-43%, median PFS 5.9 months. 10-year CheckMate-067 data (NEJM 2024) shows durable responses in overall melanoma. 5-year OS 36% for mucosal subtype. PD-L1 40% predicts favorable response.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 5.9 months, ORR 37-43% in mucosal melanoma. 5-year OS 36% for nivo+ipi vs 17% nivo alone. For locally recurrent disease without distant mets, immunotherapy may achieve durable response. However, surgery provides more reliable local control for resectable nasal septum disease.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved combination for advanced melanoma. Widely available worldwide. Standard first-line for unresectable melanoma. Could be used neoadjuvantly to downstage before definitive surgery.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 AEs in 40% of mucosal melanoma patients. Immune-related colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis prominent. Endocrinopathies may be permanent (hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency). Higher toxicity than either agent alone.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 40% supports immunotherapy response. Dual checkpoint blockade overcomes immune evasion in mucosal melanoma with lower TMB. NRAS Q61R does not affect checkpoint inhibitor efficacy. KIT WT status rules out imatinib.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dual checkpoint blockade with nivolumab (anti-PD-1) and ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) provides complementary immune activation. Ipilimumab primes naive T-cells in lymph nodes (CTLA-4 blockade), while nivolumab reinvigorates exhausted T-cells in the tumor microenvironment (PD-1 blockade). The combination achieves higher response rates than either agent alone, critical for mucosal melanoma with its immunosuppressive microenvironment.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — first-line for unresectable/metastatic melanoma

treatments.name: Salvage Re-excision + Adjuvant Proton Beam Radiation Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: Adjuvant proton RT for sinonasal mucosal melanoma: 1-year local control 88%, OS 88% (Cancer Reports 2025). Historical proton series: 5-year OS 51%, DFS 38%. Superior to photon RT for normal tissue sparing in sinonasal cavity. Multiple institutional series support adjuvant RT improving local control.

survival_benefit.rationale: Adjuvant RT reduces local recurrence by 20-30% after surgery. Proton therapy provides 5-year OS 51% and DFS 38%. For recurrent disease (already failed initial resection), adding adjuvant RT to salvage surgery is strongly recommended to prevent further recurrence. 1-year LC 88% with proton RT.

accessibility.rationale: Salvage surgery available at experienced sinonasal surgery centers. Proton therapy limited to specialized centers. Photon IMRT is widely available alternative. Insurance coverage for proton therapy may require prior authorization.

safety_profile.rationale: Proton RT well-tolerated: no Grade 3+ late toxicities in recent series. Reduced dose to orbits, brain, optic structures vs photon IMRT. Salvage surgery may be more complex after prior resection. Nasal crusting and dryness expected.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirements. Applicable regardless of NRAS/KIT/PD-L1 status. Addresses local recurrence risk which is the primary failure pattern. Melanoma is relatively radioresistant but hypofractionated/high-dose proton RT improves local control.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Salvage endoscopic surgical re-excision removes gross recurrent disease. Adjuvant proton beam RT delivers high-dose radiation to the surgical bed and at-risk margins using the Bragg peak to spare adjacent critical structures (orbits, optic nerves, brain). Proton therapy enables dose escalation in the sinonasal cavity while minimizing late toxicity to surrounding structures.

key_evidence.study_name: Adjuvant Proton RT for Sinonasal Mucosal Melanoma

treatments.availability: Available at proton therapy centers — limited number of facilities

treatments.name: Binimetinib (MEK Inhibitor) for NRAS Q61R-Mutated Disease

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 NEMO trial (n=402): binimetinib vs dacarbazine in NRAS-mutant melanoma. PFS HR 0.62, median PFS 2.8 vs 1.5 months. ORR 15.2% vs 6.8%. In immunotherapy-pretreated patients: median PFS 5.5 months. No OS benefit demonstrated. Application for FDA approval was withdrawn.

survival_benefit.rationale: Modest PFS improvement (2.8 vs 1.5 months overall; 5.5 months post-immunotherapy). No OS benefit (11.0 vs 10.1 months, HR 1.00). ORR only 15.2%. For post-immunotherapy patients, PFS of 5.5 months and DOR of 11 months suggest more durable responses. NRAS Q61R (most common hotspot) responds to MEK inhibition.

accessibility.rationale: Binimetinib is FDA approved for BRAF V600E/K melanoma (with encorafenib). Off-label use for NRAS-mutant melanoma. Oral administration. FDA approval application for NRAS indication was withdrawn. Available off-label.

safety_profile.rationale: Manageable toxicity profile: elevated CK, rash, peripheral edema, diarrhea, nausea. Grade 3-4 AEs in ~28%. Ocular toxicity (serous retinopathy) requires monitoring. Generally better tolerated than dual checkpoint inhibitor combinations.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets NRAS Q61R signaling through downstream MEK1/2 inhibition. NRAS Q61R is the most common NRAS hotspot (49% of NRAS mutations). KIT WT status rules out imatinib. MEK inhibition is the primary targeted option for NRAS-mutant melanoma.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Binimetinib is a selective, allosteric MEK1/2 inhibitor. NRAS Q61R is a constitutively activating mutation in the RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK (MAPK) pathway. Since direct NRAS inhibition is not feasible, MEK inhibition blocks the downstream signaling cascade. This reduces tumor cell proliferation and survival signals driven by the NRAS Q61R mutation.
biomarker_requirements: NRAS mutation (Q61R, Q61K, Q61L, or other activating mutations)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for BRAF-mutant melanoma (off-label for NRAS-mutant)

treatments.name: Tunlametinib (MEK Inhibitor — First Approved Targeted Therapy for NRAS Melanoma)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 trial (n=95): ORR 35.8% (IRRC-assessed), DCR 72.6%, median PFS 4.2 months, median OS 13.7 months, median DOR 6.1 months. Approved in China (March 2024) as first targeted therapy for NRAS-mutant melanoma post-PD-1/PD-L1. Not FDA approved.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 35.8% is higher than binimetinib (15.2%). Median PFS 4.2 months and OS 13.7 months. Designed for post-immunotherapy patients, directly relevant for this patient if nivo+ipi fails. Higher response rate than historical MEK inhibitors in NRAS melanoma.

accessibility.rationale: Approved in China only (NMPA, March 2024). Not FDA approved. Limited international availability. May require medical tourism or expanded access programs. Clinical trials may be available.

safety_profile.rationale: Selective MEK1/2 inhibitor with manageable toxicity. Oral dosing. Similar AE profile to binimetinib. Approved based on phase 2 data with conditional approval requiring confirmatory studies.

biomarker_match.rationale: First and only approved targeted therapy specifically for NRAS-mutant melanoma. Directly relevant to this patient's NRAS Q61R mutation. Designed for post-PD-1/PD-L1 treatment failure, fitting the potential treatment sequence.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tunlametinib is an oral, selective MEK1/2 inhibitor that blocks downstream MAPK pathway signaling driven by NRAS Q61R mutation. As the first approved targeted therapy for NRAS-mutant melanoma, it fills a critical unmet need for patients who progress on immunotherapy. Its selectivity may account for improved ORR compared to binimetinib.
treatments.availability: Approved in China (NMPA March 2024) — not FDA approved

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab Monotherapy (Anti-PD-1)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 KEYNOTE-006 established pembrolizumab for advanced melanoma. Pooled analyses show ORR >15% and median OS >11 months in mucosal melanoma with anti-PD-1 monotherapy. FDA approved. PD-L1 40% predicts above-average response.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 15-23% in mucosal melanoma with PD-1 monotherapy. Median OS ~11-15 months. Inferior to nivo+ipi combination (ORR 37-43%) but significantly better safety profile. PD-L1 40% may select for above-average response. Relevant for patients unable to tolerate combination.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for unresectable/metastatic melanoma and adjuvant melanoma (Stage IIB+). Widely available worldwide. Standard of care monotherapy option.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 AEs in only 8-17% (much lower than nivo+ipi at 40%). Manageable immune-related AEs. Well-tolerated long-term. Better quality of life than combination regimen.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 40% predicts above-average response to PD-1 monotherapy. However, mucosal melanoma generally has lower TMB and fewer neoantigens than cutaneous, reducing monotherapy efficacy. NRAS Q61R does not affect PD-1 inhibitor response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab blocks PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, restoring T-cell mediated anti-tumor immunity. PD-L1 expression of 40% in this tumor suggests active but suppressed immune response that can be reinvigorated by PD-1 blockade. Monotherapy provides disease control with favorable safety profile.

key_evidence.journal: New England Journal of Medicine / Multiple

treatments.name: Bocodepsin + Binimetinib (HDAC + MEK Inhibitor Combination — Nautilus Trial)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1b/2 Nautilus trial: ORR 30%, median PFS 7.25 months in NRAS-mutant melanoma. Combines HDAC inhibitor (bocodepsin) with MEK inhibitor (binimetinib). Promising early data exceed single-agent MEK inhibitor results. Not yet validated in phase 3.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 30% and PFS 7.25 months exceed single-agent binimetinib (ORR 15%, PFS 2.8 months). Combination approach doubles response rate and nearly triples PFS. Relevant for NRAS Q61R-driven disease after immunotherapy failure.

accessibility.rationale: Available only through clinical trial (Nautilus, NCT05340621). Both agents investigational in this combination. Limited enrollment sites.

safety_profile.rationale: Tolerable with manageable AEs. No high-grade rash (common concern with MEK inhibitors). HDAC inhibitor adds fatigue, nausea, thrombocytopenia. Early safety data encouraging.

biomarker_match.rationale: Specifically designed for NRAS-mutant melanoma. NRAS Q61R is the target mutation. Dual HDAC + MEK inhibition provides vertical pathway blockade. Rational combination for NRAS-driven tumors.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Bocodepsin (HDAC inhibitor) modifies chromatin accessibility and gene expression, inducing cell cycle arrest and apoptosis. Combined with binimetinib (MEK1/2 inhibitor), the dual approach blocks NRAS-driven MAPK signaling while simultaneously altering epigenetic regulation. HDAC inhibition may overcome MEK inhibitor resistance by reactivating tumor suppressors and modulating immune response.

treatments.name: Nivolumab + Cabozantinib (Adjuvant for Resected Mucosal Melanoma)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 Alliance A091903 trial (NCT05111574) specifically testing nivolumab +/- cabozantinib to prevent mucosal melanoma recurrence after surgery. Recruiting. No results yet. Rational combination targeting both immune checkpoint and VEGF/MET/AXL pathways.

survival_benefit.rationale: No efficacy data yet from this trial. Based on strong rationale: cabozantinib targets VEGF/MET/AXL which are commonly dysregulated in mucosal melanoma. In CheckMate-9ER (RCC), nivo+cabo improved PFS and OS vs sunitinib. Hypothesis extends to melanoma.

accessibility.rationale: Available through clinical trial at UCSF and other Alliance centers. Both drugs individually FDA approved. Combination for mucosal melanoma is investigational. May be available through off-label use.

safety_profile.rationale: Cabozantinib adds fatigue, diarrhea, hand-foot syndrome, hypertension to nivolumab immune-related AEs. Combined toxicity significant but generally manageable. Grade 3-4 AEs estimated 30-40%.

biomarker_match.rationale: Specifically designed for mucosal melanoma — directly relevant. Cabozantinib targets pathways commonly activated in mucosal melanoma (VEGF, MET, AXL). PD-L1 40% supports nivolumab component. NRAS status does not affect eligibility.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab (anti-PD-1) restores T-cell anti-tumor immunity. Cabozantinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR, MET, and AXL — all implicated in mucosal melanoma pathogenesis. Cabozantinib may reprogram the tumor immune microenvironment by reducing myeloid-derived suppressor cells and promoting T-cell infiltration, synergizing with PD-1 blockade.

treatments.name: Ribociclib + Binimetinib (CDK4/6 + MEK Inhibitor Combination)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1b/2 trial of ribociclib + binimetinib in NRAS-mutant melanoma published in Clinical Cancer Research 2022. CDK4/6 + MEK dual inhibition is considered the most promising targeted strategy for NRAS melanoma. Limited sample size but rational combination.

survival_benefit.rationale: Early-phase data show activity in NRAS-mutant melanoma. CDK4/6 inhibition addresses a key resistance mechanism to MEK inhibitor monotherapy. Combination may improve durability of response. No survival data from this combination yet.

accessibility.rationale: Both drugs individually approved for other indications (ribociclib for breast cancer, binimetinib for BRAF melanoma). Off-label combination use possible. Clinical trial access may be available.

safety_profile.rationale: Combined toxicities: myelosuppression from ribociclib, rash/GI from binimetinib. QTc prolongation monitoring required. Hepatotoxicity possible. Grade 3-4 AEs moderate.

biomarker_match.rationale: Rational targeted approach for NRAS Q61R mutation. CDK4/6 + MEK dual blockade provides vertical pathway inhibition. Considered the most promising genetically targeted strategy for NRAS-mutant melanoma.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Binimetinib inhibits MEK1/2 downstream of NRAS. Ribociclib inhibits CDK4/6, blocking cell cycle progression at G1-S transition. NRAS-mutant melanoma upregulates CDK4/6-cyclin D as a resistance mechanism to MEK inhibition. Dual blockade of MEK (proliferation signals) and CDK4/6 (cell cycle progression) provides synergistic anti-tumor effect and overcomes adaptive resistance.
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial / Off-label — both drugs individually approved for other indications

treatments.name: Salvage Re-excision + Adjuvant Photon IMRT

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple retrospective series support adjuvant RT after surgical resection of sinonasal mucosal melanoma. Surgery + RT improves locoregional control vs surgery alone. IMRT is standard technique. Meta-analysis shows 5-year OS improvement with adjuvant RT.

survival_benefit.rationale: Adjuvant RT reduces local recurrence by 20-30%. 5-year OS approximately 25% for sinonasal mucosal melanoma overall. Meta-analysis shows survival benefit with adjuvant RT. For recurrent disease, adjuvant RT after salvage surgery is strongly recommended given prior recurrence.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT widely available at all radiation oncology centers. Standard technology. No special equipment needed beyond standard linear accelerator. More accessible than proton therapy.

safety_profile.rationale: Sinonasal IMRT: risk of xerostomia, nasal dryness, visual toxicity depending on field. Late effects include nasal crusting, sinusitis, potential optic nerve damage with inadequate planning. Less conformality than proton therapy near critical structures.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirements. Standard approach regardless of molecular profile. Addresses local recurrence risk which is the primary failure pattern.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Intensity-modulated radiation therapy delivers targeted radiation to the surgical bed and regional at-risk mucosal surfaces. IMRT enables dose sculpting around critical structures (orbits, optic chiasm, brain). Addresses microscopic residual disease and reduces the 50-80% local recurrence rate inherent to mucosal melanoma.

key_evidence.study_name: Sinonasal Mucosal Melanoma Meta-analysis / Institutional Series

treatments.availability: Widely Available — standard IMRT at all radiation oncology centers

treatments.name: Temozolomide (Systemic Chemotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Temozolomide has been used historically for metastatic melanoma with ORR 10-15%. Phase 2/3 data in cutaneous melanoma. Limited specific data in mucosal melanoma. Now largely superseded by immunotherapy but remains an option for immunotherapy-refractory patients.

survival_benefit.rationale: Low ORR (10-15%). Median OS 6-8 months in advanced melanoma. Largely replaced by checkpoint inhibitors and targeted therapies. Would be considered only after failure of immunotherapy and MEK inhibitors.

accessibility.rationale: Oral chemotherapy widely available. Generic drug. Low cost. No special monitoring beyond standard blood work. Easy to administer.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well tolerated for chemotherapy: nausea, fatigue, myelosuppression (thrombocytopenia, lymphopenia). Grade 3-4 AEs in 15-20%. Oral administration convenient.

biomarker_match.rationale: No specific biomarker selection. Universal applicability but low response rate. MGMT promoter methylation may predict response but not routinely tested in melanoma.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Temozolomide is an oral alkylating agent that methylates DNA at O6 and N7 positions of guanine, triggering mismatch repair-mediated apoptosis. It crosses the blood-brain barrier, relevant for melanoma brain metastases. As systemic chemotherapy, it provides a salvage option after immunotherapy and targeted therapy failure.

treatments.name: Tebentafusp-like gp100-Targeting T-Cell Engager (Experimental — HLA-A*02:01 Restricted)

evidence_level.rationale: Tebentafusp (gp100-CD3 bispecific T-cell engager) approved for uveal melanoma. 3-year OS 27% vs 18% (HR 0.68). Targets gp100 expressed by melanocytes/melanoma cells. Mucosal melanoma application is theoretical but gp100 is expressed across melanoma subtypes. HLA-A*02:01 restriction limits eligible population.

survival_benefit.rationale: In uveal melanoma: median OS 21.6 vs 16.9 months. No data in mucosal melanoma. gp100 expression across melanoma subtypes suggests potential activity. Would be experimental/off-label for mucosal melanoma.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved only for uveal melanoma. Off-label use for mucosal melanoma would be investigational. Requires HLA-A*02:01 typing (~40-50% of Caucasians eligible). Weekly IV infusions required.

safety_profile.rationale: Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) common but manageable — grade 1-2 in most patients. Skin reactions due to on-target gp100 targeting of normal melanocytes. Rash, pruritus, vitiligo. First doses require observation.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires HLA-A*02:01 genotype (not tested in this patient). Targets gp100 antigen expressed by melanocytes. Mucosal melanoma expresses gp100. Mechanism independent of NRAS/KIT/PD-L1 status.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tebentafusp is a bispecific T-cell engager (ImmTAC) that binds gp100 peptide presented on HLA-A*02:01 on melanoma cells and CD3 on T-cells, redirecting T-cell cytotoxicity against melanoma regardless of T-cell receptor specificity. This mechanism bypasses tumor immune evasion by creating a synthetic immunological synapse between T-cells and melanoma cells.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for uveal melanoma only — experimental for mucosal melanoma

clinical_trials.title: Nivolumab Alone or With Cabozantinib to Prevent Mucosal Melanoma Return After Surgery (Alliance A091903)
clinical_trials.early_results: Specifically designed for mucosal melanoma recurrence prevention. Nivo +/- cabozantinib adjuvant. Enrollment ongoing.

clinical_trials.title: Nautilus: Bocodepsin + Binimetinib in NRAS-Mutant Metastatic Melanoma
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 30%, median PFS 7.25 months in 20 evaluable NRAS-mutant melanoma patients. Tolerable with no high-grade rash.

clinical_trials.title: Tunlametinib in Advanced NRAS-Mutant Melanoma
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 35.8%, DCR 72.6%, median PFS 4.2 months, median OS 13.7 months. First approved targeted therapy for NRAS melanoma.

clinical_trials.title: CheckMate-067: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab in Advanced Melanoma (Mucosal Subgroup)
clinical_trials.early_results: Mucosal melanoma subgroup: nivo+ipi ORR 37-43%, median PFS 5.9 months, 5-year OS 36%. 10-year overall melanoma data: nivo+ipi median OS 72 months.

clinical_trials.title: Combination Immunotherapy Approaches for Mucosal Melanoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Testing novel combinations targeting immune evasion in mucosal melanoma. Enrollment ongoing.

clinical_trials.title: Belvarafenib (Pan-RAF inhibitor) in NRAS-Mutant Advanced Melanoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Pan-RAF inhibitor targeting BRAF and CRAF downstream of NRAS. Safety and dose-finding ongoing.

combination_strategies.rationale: Triple modality addresses all failure patterns: surgery for local control, proton RT for microscopic residual disease, nivo+ipi for systemic immunosurveillance. PD-L1 40% supports immunotherapy benefit. Proton RT spares critical sinonasal structures. 50-80% recurrence rate demands aggressive multimodal approach.

Sequential immunotherapy then targeted therapy: nivo+ipi provides durable responses in 36% at 5 years. Upon progression, MEK inhibition targets NRAS Q61R signaling. Tunlametinib specifically approved post-PD-1/PD-L1 failure in NRAS-mutant melanoma. Covers both immune and molecular vulnerabilities.

Dual vertical pathway blockade for NRAS Q61R-driven disease. CDK4/6 inhibition addresses key MEK inhibitor resistance mechanism. Considered the most promising genetically targeted strategy for NRAS-mutant melanoma. For use after immunotherapy failure.

Cabozantinib targets VEGF, MET, AXL pathways commonly activated in mucosal melanoma. May reprogram immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment to enhance PD-1 blockade. Only trial specifically designed for mucosal melanoma adjuvant setting.

supportive_care.evidence: Narrow-band imaging improves detection of recurrent sinonasal mucosal melanoma by enhancing visualization of submucosal neovascularization. Serial endoscopic surveillance every 2-3 months in first 2 years recommended given 50-80% recurrence rate.
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of re-recurrence enabling timely salvage intervention. Reduced missed recurrences compared to white-light endoscopy alone. Non-invasive monitoring.

supportive_care.approach: Nasal Saline Irrigations and Mucosal Care
supportive_care.evidence: Post-surgical and post-radiation nasal irrigations with isotonic saline improve sinonasal mucosal healing, reduce crusting, and improve patient quality of life. Recommended by rhinology guidelines.
supportive_care.benefit: Improved nasal breathing, reduced crusting, maintained mucosal health. Enhanced comfort and quality of life post-treatment. Simple, low-cost intervention.

supportive_care.evidence: Mucosal melanoma has high risk of distant metastases (20-50%). PET/CT every 6 months for first 3 years, then annually, improves detection of distant disease. Brain MRI also recommended given melanoma brain metastasis risk.
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of distant metastases enabling timely systemic therapy initiation. Particularly important given NRAS Q61R-driven disease biology.

supportive_care.approach: Psychosocial Support and Rare Cancer Patient Communities
supportive_care.evidence: Sinonasal mucosal melanoma is extremely rare, causing isolation and anxiety. Patient support groups (Melanoma Research Foundation, AIM at Melanoma) provide peer support and clinical trial information. Psychological intervention reduces anxiety and depression.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced psychological distress, improved coping, access to clinical trial information. Connection with other rare cancer patients.

supportive_care.evidence: Nivo+ipi causes grade 3-4 immune AEs in 40%. NCCN and ESMO guidelines provide standardized irAE management algorithms. Early recognition and treatment with corticosteroids/immunosuppressants is critical.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevention of severe immune toxicity. Maintained treatment compliance. Reduced treatment discontinuation rates. Critical for maximizing immunotherapy benefit.
